comment_text:
I first watched a documentary on Amy a few years ago, and it really stuck with me. When I saw the new Netflix documentary about her disappearance, I watched it again. It broke my heart to see that after 26 years, her mother’s pain is still the same. I can’t imagine how hard it must be for her family to let go.
I watched the Netflix documentary again today, and now I’m thinking about her sexuality and how her parents might not have accepted it. Maybe she was trafficked, but now someone keeps checking a website on certain “special” days. It makes sense that checking the website on Amy’s birthday could be Amy herself.
But is it possible that she hated her life so much that, even after everything, she wouldn’t want to come back? I understand that she might feel shame for what she endured as a sex slave, but if it is her checking the website, I’m sure she sees the pain in her mother’s face. The premature aging in her brother’s hair. The fact that he hasn’t been able to have a family of his own because of this, and that he’s now 48. I’m sure she sees the hopelessness of a father who feels he couldn’t protect his daughter.
Is it possible that Amy was trafficked but now lives willingly in the Caribbean? If she’s still alive in 2025, she’d be 50 years old. I’m sure she has access to a phone, to the internet. Some of the working girls might have even seen videos about her on YouTube. From what I saw on camera, her family looked happy.
I have so many questions.

video_title: Amy Bradley Is Missing | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I’ve been thinking about this case for a few weeks now, ever since I watched this documentary. I’ve dabbled with a few different theories throughout that time, but today I’m confident I finally cracked it. I’m so certain I got it I’d bet money on it. I think I solved a case in three weeks that the FBI couldn’t solve in 27 years.

For starters, she obviously didn’t vanish by accidentally or intentionally going over the side. They were only about eighty feet up. I know people who can barely swim who have jumped into water from that height and came out fine, and Amy could swim like a fish. Plus, she wasn’t crazy or stupid. And no remains were found.

She was tossed over, but not alive, and probably well before 5:30 AM. All the main suspects played a role, but none of them were the main perpetrator. Yellow handed Ron a bullet, Ron loaded the gun, but it was someone else who pulled the trigger.

Yellow danced with her and shouldn’t have. He got chummy with a young woman, while as an employee he should’ve only been dancing with the old ladies. That was wrong. And he stayed up with her late, until around 3:30, which was about when Ron took her from him, with Brad, who was upset about her ditching him a half hour earlier. Between 3:00 and 3:30 Yellow and Amy pass by Chrystal and Lori, who are tired and a little loopy, no key cards with them. They of course later claimed it was more like 5:30 or 6:00, but that’s not what they initially told the FBI, which is probably why their mom who let them in their cabin wouldn’t testify. The club wasn’t even open then. 

After Ron fetched her, him and her went back to their cabin, entering at 3:35, the same time Yellow returned to his room. Brad moseyed in five minutes later, not wanting to look like a squealer. The three went out on the balcony, Ron preaching to Amy. He’s an old school Republican. He’s put up with her dating women. Now she wants a guy who’s a different race and isn’t even American. No sir, he won’t have it. He gets Brad to go to bed, while he stays up with Amy, (as confirmed in a newspaper article by Jon Pope). Once Amy is sleepy on the long chair he goes to bed, but not before locking the balcony door. He knows it’s wrong, but he just doesn’t trust her.

Amy wakes up a little later, wants to go bed and finds the balcony door locked. The family sleeping in front of her she knows she could pound on the door, but she also knows Ron did it in purpose, and she has too much pride for that. Plus, she’s not happy with Ron and Brad, telling her how to live her life. She doesn’t want to ask them for help, especially Ron, who intentionally locked the door, trapping her on the balcony. With Iva sleeping soundly she just waits out on the balcony, furious. 

Until low and behold, neighbor Wayne appears. “What’s wrong Amy,” he asks, as he leans around the balcony wondering what she’s doing out so late. “My dad locked me out” she replies. “That’s awful” he responds. “If you trust me, I can help you over onto my balcony and you can go through my room.”

She thinks about it. Normally she would never enter the 45 year old insurance agent’s room alone, but she’s mad enough at Ron to consider it. How great it would be to walk through the door of her room, and give him a piece of her mind when he wakes up. She ultimately agrees. So she pushes the table on the balcony up to the rails, grabs her cigarettes, as she might not go back right away, Wayne takes her hand and helps her onto his balcony. All this at probably a little after 4:00. 

5:30 rolls around and Iva wakes up. She sees Brad, but she doesn’t see Amy. She wakes up Ron. “Have you seen Amy?” She asks. “She’s probably on the balcony deer,” he responds. Of course he knows she is, but he doesn’t want to confess what he’s done. He walks up to the balcony pretending to look for her, and to his surprise she’s not there. “Oh God,” he thinks. “What do I do.” Knowing Iva is awake and awaiting an answer he goes back and says “she’s fine,” confirming his statement in the documentary. He says this as he thinks he can find her.

He waits twenty more minutes, until Iva is fast asleep again. Thinking she may have gone through a neighbor’s cabin, and perhaps went to Yellow’s room, he then storms out looking for her, (something he wouldn’t have done if he thought she was merely out getting coffee, as he claimed in the documentary). But he doesn’t leave before cracking open the balcony door so it doesn’t look at all like he tried to lock her out. The cracked balcony door is important to him. It also helps him cover up his guilt. Outside the room he looks everywhere, but can’t find her. Frantically he returns to his room. 

Before waking up Iva and Brad he sees the yellow polo shirt Amy was wearing the prior day. It’s not the same white t-shirt she had on when she vanished, but he decides he’ll mention that to authorities to at least make it sound somewhat like she left the balcony he locked her on. 

He wakes up Brad and Iva and the search begins. He has a large influence on how Brad tells the story from then on, because he feels very guilty about locking Amy on the balcony, and doesn’t want it coming out, much like Yellow doesn’t want his time spent with Amy coming out to his employer and wife. Ron might suspect the neighbor Wayne did something, but he’s too obsessed with pointing the finger at Yellow to even consider it. 

Sadly they don’t realize Amy’s already been tossed over. It happened at probably around 4:30. Ron of course goes with the story about seeing her on the balcony at 5:30, even though it’s not true, so the eventual search party looks in the wrong place, an no remains are found.

Anyways, it probably didn’t happen exactly like that, but I think that’s the gist of it.

comment_text:
Absolute disgusting, unethical rubbish!
Left out critical details in this missing persons case just to add drama, and steer a false narrative. 
1. The Bradley Family were fined for perjury in a Miami court in 1999 when they tried to unsuccessfully sue Royal Caribbean twice! The first was for negligence, the second for wrongful death. Yet they legally declared her dead in Virginia a few months after she went missing, and lied about witnesses who had allegedly seen her doing normal things, and not under any duress. The judge Stuart Simon said in his finding that quote They "perpetrated a fraud on the court"
2. The Bradleys were conned out of 24k, and 186k out of an Amy donation fund by Frank Jones in 1999. 
3. The next door passenger who Netflix painted as a creepy loner Wayne Breitag, was Iva and Ron's co worker at Illinois Mutual. He was a top salesman, and Ron's competition, that's probably why Iva disparaged him. 
4. Alister "Yellow" Douglas was never charged, or arrested for anything involving the disappearance of Amy Bradley. This has been the family's unfair target for 27 years. In my personal opinion the family is racist.They ruined this guy's life as a cruise ship musician. He was a man of color who was "winding" with Amy on the dance floor that's what their main problem is. 
5. The surprise phone call to try to entrap Alister by his daughter was sickening! Shame on the producers for that unethical idea. Clearly his daughter is angry he may have been unfaithful to her mother, but to set that up was bs. I hope Alister sues the producers as he has suggested he might do. 
My final thought is the most obvious explanation Occam's razor is more often than not the right one. 
I believe Amy went overboard. Either by accident, willfully, or foul play, and I also believe this seemingly loving family masking up for the public feels tremendous guilt over something. Only they know the answer...

video_title: Amy Bradley Is Missing | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
1983 bodde jag ett tag i Toulon i Frankrike, Europas största flottbas. Jag var bara 22 år gammal och hade träffat en fransk sjöman som arbetade på ett av fartygen där. Jag behövde jobb snabbt så jag fick jobb som servitris i kvarteret ""Little Chicago", ett slitet kvarter med en massa barer som sjömän från hela världen frekventerade. Där fanns prostituerade, italienska maffian, knarkhandlare, sexshoppar och en massa barer i en salig blandning. Tjejerna bakom bardiskarna var ofta men inte alltid prostituerade. Hela kvarteret var ett tufft och hårt "red light district". Jag var väldigt naiv till en början och såg inget fel eller farligt alls med att jobba där. Jag själv serverade bara drinkar och kaffe bakom en disk och tyckte jobbet var väldigt normalt, men minns att min franske pojkvän var helt galen på mig för att jag jobbade i kvarteret. Men hellre det än att leva på hans pengar tyckte jag som absolut ville vara ekonomiskt självständig, så han fick inte alls som han ville. 

Hursomhelst minns jag att jag blev varnad många gånger av både kollegor och gäster för att besöka små klädesbutiker och deras omklädningsrum i kvarteret därför att ett sånt besök kunde sluta med att man kidnappades, sövdes ner och skeppades över till Djibouti i Afrika. Jag bara skakade på huvudet och trodde inte ett ögonblick på det. Nu när jag är gammal och ser tilllbaka på det så kanske ändå att det kan ha legat någon liten sanning i det. Kvarteret var onekligen en mörk, smutsig, kriminell och farlig plats. Idag är nästan alla spår efter "Le Petit Chicago"  borta och väl är det. Jag tror faktiskt jag kan tacka min lyckliga stjärna att det slutade bra för mig. Jag flydde mer eller mindre själv från Toulon när jag hade blivit ytligt bekant med en man som jag insåg kunde vara riktigt farlig. Mina instinkter sade det till mig så väldigt klart och tydligt, så naiviteten hade gett vika för förståndet. Jag tänker ändå att Amy Bradley borde ha haft flera flyktmöjligheter under alla dessa år ändå om hon nu blev kidnappad? Om hon fortfarande lever och dessutom inte har blivit fullständigt psykiskt nedbruten av någon psykopat och saknar all kraft, mod och vilja. Mycket sorglig historia i varje fall. Hoppas sanningen om Amy kommer fram för hennes familjs skull. Vilket lidande för dem alla dessa år.

comment_text:
Senior officer with another cruise line. 23 years at sea. My opinion is she fell, jumped, or was pushed. 
The human trafficking theories, etc, are what Mission Impossible Plots are all about. 
I think the cruise director was a little harsh but correct. I can't remember the exact timeline but I believe that the father reported her missing about an hour or 2 before disembarkation. She's missing? She is an adult. Just because the father doesn't know where she is doesn't mean she's missing. She could be passed out in a stall in a bathroom. She could curled up in a  chair in the lounge or theater etc. 
You don't hold up 2400 guests and 700 guests on a whim. The Captain did exactly what I would have done, lowered the gangway and let guests do their thing. There's no crime scene, no evidence of malice.
If someone goes missing after  hours in a town of 3100 people do you issue a lock down? Nobody can come or go? Use your head people.
This human trafficking thing is implausible. The moving pieces, timing, conspirators involved. Its just ridiculous. 
We have guests that never return to the ship. We're they murdered, arrested, lost, kidnapped, died, or did they go missing on purpose? That happens. People leave their spouses, collect on life insurance, escape debts etc. 
Do we hold the ship? No. Thats life. We operate a cruise ship not a   prison

comment_text:
the first article published is on March 27, 1998 in the Richmond-Times Dispatch, where her Uncle John Noblin is quoted as saying her family believes she’s on the ship, but RC had 200 people search it. Noblin says Amy’s dad saw her at 4:30 am and notified ship officials at 6 am.
an Associated Press article on March 27, 1998 quoted Uncle John Noblin again, but this time it’s reported that he says Amy’s mom saw her last. The FBI says Amy may have fallen overboard when the ship was 10 miles from port. This is when Amy’s uncle shares they believe someone snatched her.
a Richmond-Times Dispatch article on March 28, 1998 quotes Aunt Marianne Noblin, who says Amy’s dad last saw her at 4:30 am after waking at 3 am to bring Amy and Brad back from the disco. She says Amy’s dad woke an hour later to find Amy missing. She also says that Amy was not drinking heavily and that claims of suicide are meant as a “distraction” from foul play.
an AP article on March 28, 1998 says Curaçao police reported that Amy was last seen at 4:30 am and reported missing shortly before 7 am.
an AP article on March 31, 1998 doesn’t mention the dad waking to bring the kids back to the cabin, but instead says that Amy and Brad came back from disco together. Dad says he woke just after 5 am and saw Amy, then woke an hour later as the ship was docking and she was gone.
a Richmond Times-Dispatch article on April 24, 1998 says Amy’s dad last saw her at 4:30 am after retrieving her and Brad from disco at 3 am. It says none of her shoes were missing.
a NYT article on November 16, 1998 says that the mom and dad left Amy and Brad at disco. The first mention of the swipe log is here, showing that Brad came back at 3:35 am and Amy came back at 3:40 am. Dad says he woke at 6 am and Amy was missing and alerted ship authorities at 7 am as ship was docking.
a Miami Herald article on March 24, 1999 says that Amy’s family is suing RC, claiming that she was abducted and held on the ship for days before being forced into a taxi in Puerto Rico. This article says that according to RC, Amy disappeared between 5 and 6:15 am. RC says all crew members passed voluntary polygraph tests from the FBI.
a Miami Herald article on October 26, 2000 reports how the lawsuit was dismissed by a judge because Amy’s family committed “fraud on the court” by lying to attorneys and purposefully concealing 105 witnesses who reported seeing Amy in Curaçao, who was not under duress.
a Richmond Times-Dispatch article on April 29, 2002 says that Amy’s dad last saw her at 5:15-5:30 am as the ship entered the channel leading to port. Then he says he woke again at 6 am and saw Amy missing. Amy’s mom says it’s possible Amy wore other shoes. This is the article where the claim surfaces about Yellow’s involvement and two women reported seeing Amy and Yellow in an elevator shortly before the ship docked at 6 am.
I think the most important thing here is that a judge tossed out Amy’s family’s lawsuit against RC because of the fraud they committed. This demonstrates that they are willing to lie and conceal facts to suit their narrative. Pairing that with the shifting details and timelines, I’m left with the impression that it’s a purposeful attempt to make details suit their storyline and not an accidental oversight.

My theory is that "Yellow" played the biggest role in her disappearance, and others on that ship were involved . Her mother mentioned people staring at her and trying to get her off the ship. I think when Yellow approached her to dance, he already had something planned, possibly using drugs as a way to lure her out of the ship. Maybe she had never taken drugs before and initially said no because she was with her family.

Later, after dancing and meeting with her brotherin their room, she might have changed her mind, went back to find him, and left her shoes behind, possibly to quietly sneak out without waking her parents. And that’s when the girls saw her with "Yellow" again. They probably talked, and he could have given her something or promised more at the next stop or told her he couldn’t give it to her on the ship.

She might have gone out thinking she was getting drugs, but it was actually a setup to grab her. "Yellow" had all those photos and an inconclusive lie detector test. I really believe this is what happened.

And if you ask why she hasn’t contacted anyone it's been many years. She might have a new life, maybe even children, and is either scared for their safety or feels she can't come back without risking harm to herself or her family

I suspected Yellow at first, mainly because of the eye witness accounts of him on the ship with Amy at around 6:00 on the morning she disappeared, but then I watched an interview with him from about a year ago. I learned those eye witness accounts couldn’t have been accurate. It turns out staff called him in his room at around 7:00 that morning to ask if he was with Amy, and he answered. If he was out on the ship with Amy at around 6:00 there would’ve been a record of him re-entering his room. He seemed very credible in the interview as well. I don’t think it was him.

I don’t think she committed suicide. Even if she was depressed, which all accounts indicate she wasn’t, jumping off a ship would be dumb way to do that. I don’t think she accidentally fell either. Not only were the rails really high, there would’ve almost certainly been a scream, and a plop in the water. Plus, no body was found.

While I certainly can’t prove anything, if I had to guess I’d say that weird neighbor, Wayne Breitag, had something to do with it. I looked him up, and he was convicted a few years later of smuggling big game from South Africa. He traveled alone, he strangely tried to get to know Amy by leaning around the rails on the ship, and witnesses say he had his television up loud while talking to someone in his room that morning. This on top of the fact that his demeanor didn’t seem credible to me at all, (although in all fairness that might just be how he is). Still from my perspective he should the top suspect.

comment_text:
There is a lot of nonsense in this story, and usually the simplest explanation is the right one.
First, Amy Bradley does not fit the profile of a typical sex trafficking victim. Those girls are usually a lot younger, 15 or so, and from backgrounds that make them the perfect victims, impoverished, on their own or with families that do not care about them. 
Human traffickers go after the easiest victims, they very rarely go through the trouble of kidnapping a grown up young woman from a close knit family, whom they know will move heaven and earth to find her. 
Second, the families who experience the loss of a beloved family member go through hell emotionally and mentally. As any good therapist will explain, they will go through all sorts of false hopes, delusions, and denials regarding the loss of their loved ones. It is a normal and heartbreaking human response, but it is rarely real. 
Third, after watching the Netflix special, my instinct tells me that Amy seemed happy and not the type of individual to commit suicide, but then again I did not know her personally. The most simple and realistic explanation is that unfortunately, between the hours of 5:30 and 6:30 am, she met with a bad person who probably acted inappropriately towards her, which freaked her out and her aggressor then killed her in a panic and threw her body overboard.

comment_text:
Even if it’s hard to accept, everything points to the fact that she was trafficked.
If she truly wasn’t the young woman shown on that sex site, then why didn’t anyone publicly step forward and say, “That’s not her”? Why wasn’t there a clear and transparent identification?

She was last seen around 6:00 a.m. with the bass player Douglas Alister—yet later, he was seen leaving the disco deck alone. That timing wasn’t random. Earlier, when people were still awake, coming back from the club between 2 and 3 a.m., it would’ve been too risky for him to meet with her. Too many eyes. That’s why he likely waited until 6:00 a.m.—a time when most passengers would be asleep or getting ready to disembark. That window of time provided privacy and opportunity.

It also explains why, when Amy entered their room, she stayed up a bit longer, probably trying not to raise suspicion. She went out to the balcony—maybe to pass time unnoticed—and waited for the right moment to meet with Douglas Alister without anyone seeing her leave.

The fact that she changed clothes and took her ID shows she was leaving with intention—either to meet someone or go eat. But we know she left, and we know she met with him.

This meeting happened just one hour before she was scheduled to dock on land. That detail is important. Timing is everything. If he sedated her or gave her something right before docking, she wouldn’t wake up—and it would be easier to remove her from the ship undetected.

There’s more: a man from the Navy who encountered a young woman matching her description later in a Caribbean town testified that she told him she had been trying to buy drugs—but instead, she was deprived of her freedom. This is key. It suggests she may have been lured with the promise of drugs, and then taken advantage of.

It all fits. Douglas Alister didn’t meet with her earlier after the club, because too many people were awake. He waited until 6:00 a.m., when he knew most were asleep or distracted with disembarkation prep. It was the perfect moment—not to deliver drugs, but to sedate her, exploit her vulnerability, and begin the trafficking process.

To me, it’s clear that Douglas Alister didn’t act alone. He was likely part of a trafficking ring, and someone else probably helped move the young woman—still alive—hidden inside one of the large boxes that are routinely unloaded every time the ship docks.

And then there’s the disturbing story about the daughter who later found a suitcase filled with photos of young white, Caucasian women. That’s incredibly strange. It raises serious questions. Maybe this wasn’t the first time something like this had happened—maybe it wasn’t the first time a woman traveling alone on a cruise ship vanished without a trace.

This could very well be part of a larger human trafficking operation—one that targets women traveling solo. If so, this young woman wasn’t an isolated case. She may have been one of many.

I’ve been wondering—what was the name of the website where a photo of Amy Bradley supposedly appeared, showing her as a sex worker? Was it ever determined who owned that website, or at least what country or region it was based in?

Also, the place where the Navy officer said he saw Amy—was it ever found out who the owners of that establishment were at the time? Because I believe the investigation should’ve started there. If there were armed individuals working at that bar, they were probably connected to it somehow, and it’s very likely the owners knew what was going on—especially if human trafficking was involved.

And what about the woman who said she saw Amy in a restroom? Did investigators ever check who owned that establishment at the time? Did they interview the owners? Someone out there probably knows something. If Amy really was in that kind of underground bar, then tracking down the owners could’ve led to uncovering a trafficking network—if that’s what this really was.

Anyway, that Navy officer—just because he wanted to keep his honorable discharge and avoid being removed from his position—I honestly don’t know how he’s lived with himself all these years. For not speaking up sooner... I truly don’t know how he lives in peace.

Of course, I want to be clear that I am not a direct witness. These are my personal conclusions based on everything I’ve seen, read, and researched. From all the information I’ve gathered, this is what I understand may have happened—or did happen. I share it because I believe it’s important to speak up when something doesn’t feel right, especially when a life may have been taken from someone in such a tragic and calculated way.

CRITICAL Questions the documentary didn't ask (and should have)
1. Did Yellow (Alister Douglas) Disembark the ship in Curacao. If so, WHEN did he Disembark the ship (at what time) This is an absolutely CRUCIAL Question if he is to be considered a suspect.
2. Was Yellow (Alister Douglas) ALONE or with others when he Disembarked? (If so, how many people, who were they)
3. Was Yellow (Alister Douglas) carrying any large suitcases when he Disembarked the ship?
4. Did Yellow assist in the search aboard every nook and cranny of the cruise ship or was he already off the boat at this time?
5. If the FBI Considers him a suspect, HOW do they believe he could have carried out the kidnapping and trafficking? Did he drug her, drag her down stairs, stuff her in a case and roll her out? Did she walk out under her own power?
Why didn't the documentary address this? 
******************************
If I could see Netflix do another series like this on any person it would be 23 year old Jeffrey John Zoltkowski, who went to Molokai in Hawaii in March 31 1993, walked into the mountains, flagged down a helicopter, gave the helicopter his bag, and was then never seen again. Molokai isn't that big of an island and there are ZERO dangerous animals there, furthermore, the population is around 5500 people, and there is one tiny airport. If he had left, people would have seen him leave, if he had died in the woods, someone would have found him by now. WHERE IS JEFFREY JOHN ZOLTKOWSKI? (My second, scarier option would be Tami Lynn Leppert, but everything about that case scares the sh*t out of me and it's unsolvable at this point 1981 was a long time ago and there were too many active serial killers in the area where she disappeared.)
****************************

comment_text:
Something very similar happened in Italy a few years ago: brother and sister were travelling on a ferry to visit family and overnight the girl disappeared, leaving her jacket on a rail on one of the decks.
The whole ship was searched and the parents feed a media frenzy saying that their daughter was killed by the crew or kidnapped.
It turned out from her phone records that she was depressed and she threw herself overboard after splitting up with her boyfriend.
Obviously the body was never recovered.

Just saying
